name: web-content-analyzer
description: Analyze web page content length and recommend optimal AI reading strategy. Prevents context overflow.

before-fetch-url.description: Call before web_fetch on a target URL (not search result pages themselves — those are usually <3000 chars). Use when about to read a full article, README, or documentation page.
before-fetch-url.typical_use: AI found a URL from search results and wants to read the full content

before-browse-text.description: Call before $B text command ONLY. $B snapshot returns Accessibility Tree (naturally compact, ~800 chars), does NOT need pre-check. $B text returns full page text (potentially 25K+ chars), DOES need pre-check.
before-browse-text.output: assessment level + alternative strategy if danger
before-browse-text.typical_use: AI wants to extract full text from a web page via browse

after-ocr-extraction.description: Call after OCR extracts text from multi-page documents. Single-page OCR (invoices, receipts) typically <500 chars — no pre-check needed. Multi-page PDF (5+ pages) can produce 3500+ chars — pre-check recommended.
after-ocr-extraction.typical_use: OCR extracted 30K chars from a 50-page PDF, need to decide how to feed to AI

rationale.safe_3000: Single tool return limit is ~2-8K tokens. 3000 chars × 1.5 = 4500 tokens, within safe range.
rationale.warning_10000: 10000 chars × 1.5 = 15000 tokens, approaching single return limit. May be truncated.

multi-search-engine.relationship: NOT for search result pages (those are ~3000 chars, safe). Use AFTER search, BEFORE reading target URLs found in search results.
multi-search-engine.when: AI clicks a search result and wants to read the full page
multi-search-engine.how: analyze the target URL before fetching, recommend strategy
multi-search-engine.value: HIGH — search results often lead to long documentation pages
multi-search-engine.example: const report = await analyzeWebPage(targetUrl); if (report.assessment.level==='danger') use progressive dialogue

browse.relationship: Only relevant for $B text, NOT $B snapshot. Snapshot returns Accessibility Tree (~800 chars, always safe). $B text returns full page text (potentially 25K+ chars).
browse.when: AI plans to use $B text to extract full page content
browse.how: pre-check URL, if danger level recommend $B snapshot -i or $B snapshot -d 2 instead
browse.value: MEDIUM — $B text is less commonly used than $B snapshot
browse.example: const report = await analyzeWebPage(url); if (report.assessment.level==='danger') { use '$B snapshot -i' } else { '$B text' is safe }
browse.note: browse uses headless Chromium. Most browse workflows use snapshot (compact). $B text is the edge case.

ocr-document-processor.relationship: Only relevant for multi-page documents. Single-page OCR output is typically <500 chars (safe). Multi-page PDF can produce 30K+ chars (danger).
ocr-document-processor.when: OCR extracts text from 5+ page documents
ocr-document-processor.how: use calculateStats(ocrText) to check output length, chunk if danger level
ocr-document-processor.value: MEDIUM — only for multi-page document scenarios
ocr-document-processor.example: const stats = calculateStats(ocrText); if (stats.charCount > 10000) { chunk and process page by page }
ocr-document-processor.note: Requires calculateStats(text) API, not analyzeWebPage(url). OCR output is already text, not a URL.

ocr-super-surya.relationship: Same as ocr-document-processor. Only relevant for multi-page document output.
ocr-super-surya.when: Surya OCR extracts text from 5+ page documents
ocr-super-surya.how: use calculateStats(ocrText) to check output length
ocr-super-surya.value: LOW — Surya is typically used for single images, not multi-page documents

find-skills.relationship: NOT a technical integration. find-skills output is ~1500 chars (always safe). This is a DISCOVERY channel — users can find and install our skill through find-skills.
find-skills.when: user asks 'is there a skill for web content analysis' or 'how to avoid context overflow'
find-skills.how: find-skills recommends our skill for installation

analyzeWebPage.output: report object with statistics, assessment, recommendations
analyzeWebPage.use_when: you have a URL and want to know if it's safe to read directly

calculateStats.output: stats object with charCount, tokenEstimate, screenEstimate
calculateStats.use_when: you already have text (e.g., OCR output) and want to check if it fits in context

formatReport.use_when: you want to show the analysis result to the user
